text: Cognitive offloading extends beyond to-do lists. Architects and composers have long used external representations — sketches, chord charts, physical models — not merely to record ideas but to think through them. Extended Mind theorists Andy Clark and David Chalmers proposed in 1998 that cognition is not bounded by the skull: when an external object reliably functions as part of a cognitive process, it is genuinely part of that mind. A notebook that holds your half-formed argument is not a backup copy of your thought; it is an active extension of it. Offloading to the right external medium can therefore expand the effective size of working memory beyond biological limits.
question: According to Clark and Chalmers' Extended Mind thesis as described in the passage, what qualifies an external object as part of a person's mind?

options.text: When it is created by the person and stored in a personal location
When it contains information the person cannot hold in biological memory
When it reliably functions as part of a cognitive process
When it is accessed more than once during a single work session
